next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 10

The room had gotten too cold to stay fully asleep in. June had kicked the duvet halfway off sometime before dawn, and the strip of hotel sheet across Leela's thigh felt damp where sweat had dried after the sex and then cooled under the vent. When she moved, the skin along the outside of her hip reminded her about the carpet. From the bathroom came toothbrush noise, then running water, then June clearing her throat like she resented even that.

On the desk, both laptops were open where they'd gone dead in the middle of the night. June's still displayed the thumbnail pane of `ruderal_pitch_v3.pdf`, frozen at a slide with a blue-orange benchmark plot that Leela knew too well now to look at casually. It had felt almost cinematic yesterday, in the bad fluorescent hallway outside Ballroom C, to find the deck at all. This morning it looked uglier and more ordinary. It was a PDF with a date stamp, a thing that would either survive contact with policy or get rinsed down to misunderstanding.

The bathroom door opened. June came out in yesterday's T-shirt and hotel socks, her blunt-cut hair shoved back with wet fingers. There was a bright scrape on her collarbone that Leela knew she had made. June saw her see it and did not pull the shirt up.

"You're awake," June said.

Leela pushed herself up against the headboard. "Barely."

"Good." June picked up the room-service notepad from the nightstand, tore off the page with the breakfast menu printed on the back, and sat cross-legged at the foot of the bed. "Let's figure out whether this is real or whether he's just such a fuc...
open_hooks_to_carry: Use conference dislocation to make the characters slightly stranger to themselves than they are on campus.
Make June's discovery of the deck factual and undeniable rather than intuitive or melodramatic.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...wn, enough to breathe through next month, enough to make the lie feel functional.

"Amma."

"Pay it tonight before you decide to become noble," Anita said. She turned back to the sink and picked up the last steel bowl. "And don't send it back. I will only send it again and be irritated."

Leela did not say thank you right away because thank you would have made the whole transaction sound cleaner than it was. She stood at the counter with the notification still bright on the screen, the digits reflected faintly in the dark window over the sink. Behind her, the diyas at the front step burned low and steady. In the sink Anita rinsed soap from the bowl, set it on the rack, reached automatically for the next thing that needed doing.

Leela opened her banking app. Relief moved through her first, plain as hunger. After that came humiliation, and after that something meaner: the knowledge that her body could not entirely distinguish between being loved and being bailed out. She did not return the money. She did not even hover over the option. She looked at the new balance, thought of the minimum due on the eighteenth, thought of groceries, train fare back, the little strip of life that twelve hundred dollars could hold in place, and let the transfer settle.

On the counter her phone lit once more with Mohit's name, another text arriving into the house as if he owned some corridor there. Leela turned the screen facedown without reading it.

"Put the laddus away before they dry out," Anita said.

Leela slid the sweets back into the steel tin with her oiled fingers and kept the money.
state_deltas_required: June and Leela need to survive a regional conference, protect June's reputation, and figure out why Mohit keeps disappearing from the official program.
The students gain concrete evidence of theft, and that night Leela and June sleep together, transforming the emotional stakes of every later betrayal.
